food_subgroup: Offal and organ meat dishes — cold mixed preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pig ear food product class; no specific entry for the cold-mixed Sichuan preparation exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 covers porcine offal generically; no specific code for cold-mixed spicy pig ear salad. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pig ear food product or this preparation style.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Spicy Pig Ear Salad is approximately 150 g, representing a small appetizer plate as commonly served in Chinese restaurants or as a cold side dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork, ear, fresh, raw (NDB No. 10193) and composite estimation for cold-mixed preparation with chili oil and condiments
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Spicy Pig Ear Salad (Liangban Zhu Er) exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB. Values are adapted from USDA pork ear raw data (NDB 10193) combined with USDA entries for chili oil, soy sauce, black vinegar, garlic, and sesame seeds to reflect the cooked and dressed preparation. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce brine. Fat is elevated due to chili oil dressing. Values are consistent with the input enriched_json record and are plausible for this preparation. Input confidence 0.9 retained as medium given the composite estimation basis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of High-Protein, Low-Carbohydrate Meat-Based Dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked pork offal with condiments
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spicy Pig Ear Salad. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked pork preparations. Carbohydrate content is 2.5 g per 100 g, predominantly from condiments (sugar, black vinegar). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 2.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.56, rounded to 0.6. This is consistent with the input record's estimated GI of 10; a value of 15 is used as a more conservative and defensible analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce brine and chili oil dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Spicy Pig Ear Salad due to its high sodium content of 450 mg per 100 g; a 150 g serving contributes approximately 675 mg sodium, representing nearly 30% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g) and high protein (18.5 g per 100 g) supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spicy Pig Ear Salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low carbohydrate content and high protein, which minimise postprandial blood glucose excursions; however, the high sodium content warrants caution if hypertension is a comorbidity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (14.2 g per 100 g) including saturated fat (~3.8 g) and high sodium from soy sauce brine
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Spicy Pig Ear Salad in small portions and infrequently, as the combination of moderate saturated fat and high sodium may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; choosing preparations with reduced soy sauce and chili oil is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High collagen content from pig ear cartilage and skin
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pig ears are rich in collagen which may support joint tissue; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor personal tolerance to high spice levels and offal, as these may act as individual triggers.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Pig Ear Salad (凉拌猪耳, Liángbàn Zhū'ěr) is a classic Chinese cold appetizer originating from Sichuan and Northern Chinese culinary traditions. Pig ears are cleaned and slow-simmered in a fragrant master brine of soy sauce, ginger, star anise, and cinnamon until tender yet retaining a firm, cartilaginous bite. Once cooled, they are thinly sliced to reveal alternating layers of gelatinous skin and crisp cartilage, then tossed in a bold dressing of Sichuan chili oil, black vinegar, minced garlic, and sugar. The dish is typically garnished with fresh coriander and toasted sesame seeds. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, spicy, tangy, and slightly sweet, with a distinctive dual texture of soft collagen-rich skin and crisp cartilage. It is a quintessential 'jiu cai' (drinking food) served as an appetizer or accompaniment to alcoholic beverages at Chinese meals. Nutritionally, it is high in protein (18.5 g per 100 g) and collagen, moderate in fat (14.2 g per 100 g), very low in carbohydrates (2.5 g per 100 g), and high in sodium (450 mg per 100 g) due to the soy sauce brine. It is suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns but should be limited by those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and fat content.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Spicy Pig Ear Salad exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, or other approved primary sources. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA pork ear raw data combined with condiment entries, consistent with the input enriched_json record (confidence 0.9). Medium confidence is assigned due to the composite estimation basis and absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central pork ear (NDB 10193) and condiment entries; no exact approved-source match for the cold-mixed Sichuan preparation exists. 2. Saturated fat (3.8 g), monounsaturated fat (5.5 g), and polyunsaturated fat (3.2 g) are estimated from USDA pork ear and chili oil composition data; these are plausible but carry additional uncertainty. 3. cholesterol_mg set to -1: detailed cholesterol data for this specific cooked and dressed preparation is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central for the composite dish. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture data for the cold-mixed dressed preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 5. GI estimated at 15 (low) using University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked pork preparations; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 6. The input cultural dietary_tags incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'vegan'; these have been corrected to false as the dish contains pork. 7. Duplicate portion entry in input has been deduplicated to a single 150 g serving. 8. FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact code exists for this specific preparation.
